storm.summary_layperson: Thunderstorms are likely to fire this afternoon in the extreme heat, but nothing is on radar yet and the cool Long Island Sound should take the punch out of any storms before they reach the cove — inland towns are more likely to get the heavy rain.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Sound water (71°F vs. 88°F air — a 17°F gap) maintains a strong stable marine layer that typically chokes incoming storms at the shoreline.
storm.detail_html:
Radar is currently clean — the 12:11 PM KOKX scan shows no storm cells within tracking range, and the arrival engine reports nothing in the approach sector. The threat today is storms that haven't formed yet: NWS Upton has showers and thunderstorms likely after 2 PM (70% chance), fired by an extreme-heat air mass — high near 96°F with heat index values to 104°F, and an Extreme Heat Warning in effect until 9 PM.

Instability is real but not extreme: HRRR CAPE is 1,290 J/kg (RAP runs hotter at 1,840 J/kg) with 0–6 km shear around 33 kt from the west. That's the gray zone — enough organization for vigorous cells, but below the ~35 kt gate where storms reliably intensify at the coast. Notably, HRRR decays CAPE quickly this evening (a few hundred J/kg after 6 PM), so the storm window is the afternoon heat peak, not the night.

The dominant local modifier is a strong marine stable layer: Sound water is 71°F against 88°F air — a 17°F differential — and the pipeline's Sound-effect likelihood is high with the protective mechanism active. Storms approaching on the westerly steering flow must cross this layer, and at 1,290–1,840 J/kg they are well below the ~3,000 J/kg punch-through threshold.

Most likely outcome for the cove: storms fire inland over the hot interior mid-to-late afternoon; the cove sees gusty westerly outflow and possibly a brief shower, while the heavy rain and lightning stay over towns a few miles north. This is the classic cove-spared, inland-soaked pattern when the marine layer is this strong.

What would change the call: CAPE verifying toward the RAP number (1,840+) with shear climbing toward the 35–40 kt the extended HRRR hours show would erode the marine-layer protection — an organized line coming off the NYC metro area would be the punch-through scenario. Watch for cells consolidating to the west and for sustained 35+ kt gusts at upstream APRS stations; either would shift this to a direct-hit call.

wind.regime_label: Synoptic westerly on an extreme-heat day

hourly.shift_note: steady W — cove running ~3 kt above model (RYC avg 11 kt now)
W/WNW holding; gusty outflow possible if storms fire

wind.tactics_blurb: W/WNW 8–10 kt through the evening with the cove running a few knots above model, easing gradually after sunset (~8:22 PM) — start center with a slight right lean toward the WNW pressure, and be ready to bail if a thunderstorm cell approaches: gusty outflow can arrive ahead of any rain.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today is Regime B-moderate — gradient-only westerly, despite a huge thermal setup. The inland–water differential is 16.6°F (91°F inland vs. 71°F Sound), which would normally scream sea breeze, but only 22% of coastal stations are blowing southerly (CT coast 44%, LI north shore 0%) and HRRR keeps the flow W–WNW all evening. The westerly gradient has organized the boundary layer and the classic SSW thermal never established. Mean authoritative wind is 8.7 kt — squarely B-moderate.

Model/obs divergence is significant and worth knowing: HRRR forecasts W 269° while the western-LIS ensemble reads NW 318° — a 49° gap with direction agreement at just 0.46, typical of a superheated midday boundary layer full of local thermals. RYC itself reads NW 323° at 6.8 kt (10-min average 10.9 kt, gusts 16). Direction confidence is low right now; expect the field to settle toward W/WNW as the afternoon mixes out. Trust RYC for the current hour and treat forecast directions as ±20°.

A forecast-detail caveat: the 15Z HRRR cycle's hour-by-hour data ends at 5 PM local. The 6–10 PM numbers above are built from HRRR's extended outlook (evening speeds 3.5–11.2 kt, mean 7.2) plus the B-moderate evening rules — treat them as a trend, not gospel.

Cove geography in W/WNW flow: wind channels down the Sound from the NYC direction — steady direction overall, but with holes near the CT (northern/eastern) shoreline and the best pressure mid-cove toward the mouth. Course geometry is fairly symmetric; favor center starts with a slight right-side lean toward the dominant WNW source. No thermal collapse cliff tonight — B-moderate gradient holds through sunset (~8:22 PM) and just softens 1–2 kt after dark.

The wildcard is convection: NWS has thunderstorms 70% likely after 2 PM. If a cell fires upwind, the first sign on the water will be a sharp W gust line well ahead of any rain — the marine layer tends to strip the rain but pass the outflow. 10–12 kt in the cove is powered-up, good racing for dinghies; a storm-outflow gust to 25+ would be a different evening. Keep an eye west.

conditions.sky: Mostly Sunny then Showers And Thunderstorms Likely

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 offline (UConn ERDDAP unreachable) — over-water wind and sea-breeze depth signal unavailable
KISP (Islip) wind sensor down — LI south shore coverage degraded
KPVD (Providence) anemometer data null — backdoor sentinel at 2 of 3 stations (out of season, low impact)
GW4034 APRS stale 830 min — excluded from ensemble
HRRR 15Z cycle hourly detail ends 5 PM local — 6–10 PM wind rows synthesized from the extended outlook, lower confidence
Direction agreement across the ensemble is 0.46 (midday thermal mixing) — forecast directions are ±20°
